schools.summary: 'The first day of the week' (cf. Mark 16:2; Luke 24:1); John relates only what he personally knew, harmonizing with the Synoptic visit of the women.
Calvin/Henry: the resurrection being the most important article of faith, the Evangelists carefully amass proofs of it (Acts 1:3).
Chrysostom: Mary, the Sabbath over, could rest no longer but came early to seek consolation at the grave.
John follows only Mary Magdalene, who left before seeing the angels; 'the other disciple' is John (13:23; 19:26); she assumes the body removed, not raised.
Gill: she could say 'taken away' only having looked in; her words breathe distress, not yet faith.

themes: they have taken away the Lord

schools.summary: The vivid eyewitness account peculiar to John (vs Luke's mention of Peter only, 24:12); the two start instantly for the tomb.
Calvin: their great zeal outran their faith — a seed of faith remained, quenched for a time; they expected no risen Lord but investigated the report.
A plain reading: the younger John simply ran faster; not an allegory of love vs zeal, but the incidental detail of an eyewitness.
John stoops and sees the linen clothes lying (eyewitness present tense); Lightfoot details the burial-cave.
Calvin: the grave-clothes are evidential 'spoils' — a body removed by friend or foe would not be stripped and the wrappings left.
Clarke: John did not enter, satisfied the body was gone, nor seize the linen as a relic — superstition's day not yet come.
Peter impulsively enters and 'beholds' (theōrei, careful notice) where John only glanced (blepō) from without — an eyewitness distinction of verbs.
Wesley: the ministering angels folded up the napkin and linen clothes when he rose.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns the use of Scripture and does not bear on v.6.

schools.summary: The head-napkin rolled up by itself 'in orderly fashion, no haste' — eyewitness detail refuting the theft story (Mt 28:13).
Calvin: the separate napkin refutes the relic of a single 'holy winding-sheet'; the order signals an unhurried rising, not robbery.
Robertson: John 'saw into the meaning' better than Peter — likely an incipient resurrection-faith from the orderly grave-clothes.
Calvin: 'believed' is not merely Mary's report of removal but the beginning of faith that Christ was risen.
Clarke/Wesley: he believed only that the body was gone, as Mary said, not yet that he was risen (cf. v.9).

debates.question: What did John believe on seeing the empty tomb?

sides.position: only that the body had been removed

schools.summary: They had not grasped the Scripture (chiefly Ps 16:10; cf. Acts 2:25-32) nor recalled Christ's foretellings, forgotten in their grief.
Calvin: warned by the strange sight they begin to sense something Divine, though far from clear knowledge; faith dawns from sight before Scripture.
They return 'to themselves' (their lodgings); John, who had taken Mary home (19:27), hurries to tell her; their object accomplished.
Gregory/Chrysostom: the men depart, but Mary, her soul burning with love, lingers weeping at the sepulchre.
Calvin: their faith was still doubtful — a confused remembrance like a trance, until more fully confirmed.
A pathetic eyewitness picture — Mary, who had gone and returned, now lingers alone weeping and stooping to look again into the tomb.
Henry: the constancy and fervency of Mary's affection — Christ appeared first to her (Mk 16:9).
Calvin: probably other women were with her; her persevering love is the occasion of the first appearance.
A distinct vision to Mary (Matthew/Mark one angel, Luke/John two); Lightfoot: head and feet recall the cherubim at the mercy-seat (Ex 25:18-19).
Calvin: amazing forbearance — Christ honours Mary's faults by sending his angels and at length revealing himself.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns false Christs and does not bear on v.12.

schools.summary: Mary alone now ('I know not', singular); her grief of v.2 still possesses her — 'my Lord' — supposing the body removed by friends.
Calvin: the angels' question is reproof mingled with comfort — she had reason to rejoice, not weep, for he was risen.
She turns and sees Jesus but, not expecting him and in dim twilight, does not recognize him (cf. Luke 24:16).
Calvin: not a changed body but the fault in her eyes — a divine restraining, as with the Emmaus disciples (Luke 24:16).

themes: saw Jesus but knew him not

schools.summary: 'Sir' (not Lord) — she supposes him the gardener/bailiff; her devotion would carry the body herself ('Love feels no load').
Calvin: she addresses him as a labourer of low condition — her view of the matter is wholly earthly.
'Mary' in the familiar tone, 'Rabboni' (my Teacher/Master); the first words of the risen Lord spoken to her devoted love.
Calvin: by a single word he corrects her mistake, addressing his disciple by name; Gill: the Shepherd knows his own and calls them by name (10:3).
'Cease clinging to me' (not 'touch not') — he let the women hold his feet (Mt 28:9); a redirection from the old fellowship to the coming ascension.
Gill/Calvin: 'my Father and your Father, my God and your God' — his by nature, theirs by grace; the disciples now named his 'brethren.'
Cyril: the Son is in all things like the Father, begotten Life of Life — guarding the distinction in the shared 'Father.'

debates.question: Why forbid Mary to touch him, yet later invite Thomas?

sides.position: 'cease clinging' — do not detain me; the fellowship is changed
Mary did not doubt (so needed no touch), unlike Thomas

schools.summary: Mary announces 'I have seen the Lord' (perfect) plus his message; the first herald of the resurrection, recorded by a fellow-disciple.
Clarke: the disciples could not believe her (Mk 16:11), treating it as troubled imagination; Gill: she obeyed cheerfully.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns the feeding of the five thousand and does not bear on v.18.

schools.summary: The evening of resurrection Sunday; doors shut for fear (not necessarily passed through); between v.18 and here fall the guard's bribe and Emmaus (Mt 28:11-15; Lk 24:13-35).
Chrysostom: he comes promptly lest they grieve; Bede: the repeated 'Peace' confirms, he who made the two one.
Calvin: providentially all assembled for surer proof; Henry: showing himself alive is the infallible proof (Acts 1:3) — the promised peace (14:27; 16:33).
The unglorified body retains the nail- and spear-marks — proof of bodily resurrection against the 'spirit only' and Docetic views (John 19:34).
Calvin: the wounds are kept because he rose 'not for himself but for us' — proof and abiding memorial of redemption.
Sent as the Father sent the Son — same authority, Spirit, and errand; Christ their model and still bearing the Father's commission.
Calvin: he formally instals them in their office; Wesley: peace in the soul is the foundation of a true minister's mission (2 Cor 4:1; Heb 3:1).

themes: as the Father sent me, so send I you

schools.summary: A symbolic in-breathing echoing God's breath into Adam (Gen 2:7; Ezek 37:9) — representing the Spirit's nature and source.
Wesley: the Spirit out of Christ's fulness, 'an earnest of Pentecost'; Cyril: the fellowship of the Spirit bestowed on the apostles.
Calvin: he equips them by the Spirit's grace for a task beyond human capacity.

debates.question: Is this the giving of the Spirit, or an earnest before Pentecost?

sides.position: a partial bestowal / earnest of Pentecost
a real symbolic impartation echoing the new creation

schools.summary: A declarative, gospel-bound power — God alone forgives; the apostles declare reconciliation; to claim more is the mark of antichrist (Calvin: inseparable from teaching).
Wesley: remitted to the penitent believer, retained to the impenitent — according to the tenor of the gospel.
Perfect 'are forgiven' = heaven ratifies the declared word; given to all apostles alike, no Petrine primacy (Mt 16:19; 18:18).

debates.question: What is the nature of the power to remit/retain sins?

sides.position: declarative/ministerial through gospel preaching, to all alike
a priestly power to absolve (rejected as antichrist's claim)

schools.summary: Thomas 'Didymus' (twin), the desponding one (11:16; 14:5), absent from the gathering; 'the twelve' still used though Judas is dead.
Clarke: absenting himself, he lost the precious opportunity and blessing — Christ is present where two or three gather (Mt 18:20).
Calvin: his obstinate unbelief is related that the faith of the godly may be more fully confirmed.
Thomas refuses the testimony of ten eyewitnesses (who echo Mary's 'we have seen the Lord'), demanding sight and touch with a triple negative condition.
Calvin: a 'sensual judgment' at variance with faith — wishing to be wise from his own understanding; even Moses was so charged (Lightfoot).
'After eight days' = the next Sunday evening (inclusive reckoning); the scene as in v.19 but with Thomas — the pattern of the weekly first-day meeting.
Chrysostom: because one, Thomas, would examine him, Christ condescends and exhibits his wounds.
Calvin: he readily yields to Thomas's improper request, showing how earnestly he seeks to overcome unbelief; Henry marks the second appearance.
Jesus turns to Thomas, revealing knowledge of his exact words (v.25) — itself convicting — and bids him 'stop being faithless, but believing.'
Clarke: infinite compassion accommodating an obstinate but sincere disciple; probably Thomas did reach out, his doubt deeply rooted.
'My Lord and my God' is direct address, not exclamation; Jesus accepts it as worship — a clear proof of Christ's deity.
Clarke refutes the Socinian reading (addressed to the Father, condemned in Theodore of Mopsuestia); Wesley: the most explicit confession of his Godhead yet.
Probably 'hast thou believed?' — sight convinced Thomas; the higher blessing falls on those who believe without seeing (1 Pet 1:8).
Calvin: Christ blames Thomas's slowness — needing the senses is at variance with faith's nature; the beatitude commends believing on testimony.
Many signs of the whole ministry (2:23; 4:45; 12:37) are unrecorded (cf. 21:25); the narrative is selective and drawing to a close.
Calvin: John testifies he related only some of a large number, lest readers think the account exhaustive.
Henry: what is written was committed to writing 'that all might believe.'
The Gospel's stated purpose — that readers may believe Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and have life; present subjunctive = keep on believing.
Calvin: John wrote what is abundantly sufficient to confirm faith, answering vain curiosity that craves more.
Clarke: full proof of Christ's divinity as Messiah, that every believer might have eternal life.

themes: Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God
